paragraphs: Political alignment and (Mis)allocation of public resources Bishal K Chalise ∗ May 2025

Partisan bias favoring local politicians aligned with the ruling national party can re- sult in the misallocation of public resources; however, the distributional consequences of such bias remain unclear. We examine this question in the context of disaster re- covery allocation in Nepal. Utilizing a close-election regression discontinuity design and rich administrative data from an earthquake house reconstruction program, we demonstrate that ruling-party-aligned local politicians deliver more aid to their con- stituents compared to those aligned with the opposition party. Ruling politicians enroll 40 percent more new beneficiaries, but this effect is concentrated in areas with greater demand for reconstruction aid: poor rural regions and locations heavily impacted by the earthquake. This redistribution is facilitated by the program’s grievance redressal system that allows local politicians to use their discretionary power to better respond to demands of the earthquake-affected households. Consequently, the incumbent can- didate and their party get rewarded with a higher vote share in the next election, but not sufficiently enough to affect likelihood of winning re-election.

Keywords: ruling politicians, political alignment, disaster aid, favouritism JEL Codes: H72, Q54 ∗ Chalise (email: b.chalise@deakin.edu.au ), Tribhuvan University, Nepal and Deakin University, Aus- tralia 1

A significant body of work in both economics and political science has documented bias in the allocation of public resources along partisan lines in multi-level govern- ments across various contexts. Examples include the preferential allocation of federal grants to municipalities whose mayors are affiliated with the president’s party in Brazil (Brollo & Nannicini, 2012) and those aligned with upper-tier governments in Portugal (Migueis, 2013) and in Spain (Curto-Grau et al., 2018; Sol´e-Oll´e & Sorribas-Navarro, 2008),  systematic over-assignment of environmentally protected areas in municipalities controlled by opposition mayors in Brazil (Mangonnet et al., 2022), favorable alloca- tion of public works funds by central parliamentarians to co-partisan state legislators in India (Bohlken, 2018), increases in discretionary subsidies to co-aligned municipal- ities by the central government in South Korea (Lee et al., 2024), a higher likelihood of disaster declarations in counties with co-partisan governors (Schneider & Kunze, 2023) and more federal outlays to congressional districts with co-partisan legislators (Berry et al., 2010) by US presidents, among many others. Nearly all of these studies document biases in government transfers and resource distribution that arise when the national or federal party in power aligns with the affiliation of the local politician, often to bolster support from their core voters or reward local party affiliates at the expense of inefficient distribution of the resources. In this paper, we extend the liter- ature by examining if the local politicians in electoral democracies help mitigate the misallocation of public resources?

We shed light on this question in the context of disaster reconstruction aid spending in a nascent constitutional democracy —Nepal —that underwent a sequence of disaster, reconstruction and election events. After a protracted civil war and the abolition of the monarchy, Nepal began drafting a new constitution in 2008 with the goal of federalizing its political and administrative framework. In 2015, just four months before the new constitution was ultimately adopted, the country was hit by a devastating earthquake that claimed 9,000 lives and damaged around one million homes. In response, the Earthquake Housing Reconstruction Program (EHRP) was created as the primary mechanism for providing reconstruction aid to those whose homes suffered damage or destruction due to the earthquake. While the reconstruction process was ongoing, in 2017, Nepal conducted local elections under the new constitution for the first time 1 . These elections were held across 6,743 wards (semi-autonomous sub-municipal areas), resulting in over 36,000 local politicians taking office in new constitutionally mandated roles. As a result, these newly elected local leaders gained increased oversight of the EHRP, which, prior to the elections, had primarily been managed by bureaucrats appointed at the federal level.

This setting is particularly well-suited for examining the role of political alignment of local leaders on development outcomes for at least four reasons. First, local elections in Nepal were held after 20 years with no elected representative in office in between. As a result, the EHRP program that we examine is relatively free of biases in allocation arising from pre-existing local incumbent leaders. This feature also implies that unlike previous studies in the literature, Nepali political context provides a cleaner context in which to examine the role of politicians’ influence. This unique setting helps circumvent potential issues that could arise due to existing incumbent local leaders and political power centres. Second, the EHRP program constitutes a significant reconstruction pro- gram, placing it among the largest owner-driven post-disaster housing reconstruction cash transfer program globally ( World Bank, 2021 ). The program supported affected households in reconstructing their private residential houses by providing a cash trans- fer of USD 3,000—about three times the annual per capita income 2 . Third, the program was set up through World Bank aid and had a number of checks as a part of the aid conditionalities to minimize leakages and potential “fake” beneficiaries. For instance, 1 Elections to the constituent assembly, which also acted as national parliament, had been held before that in Nov 2013. 2 The average construction cost of a rural house was estimated to be about NRs. 400,000 to NRs. 500,000 (USD 4000 to USD 5000). The estimate for urban areas in Kathmandu Valley, was between NRs. 1 to NRs. 1.5 million (USD 10000 to USD 15000) at a minimum.

beneficiaries received grant monies directly to their bank accounts rather than through any government intermediaries and the aid was received in installments based on mon- itoring milestones in the house reconstruction process. This helps to rule out political corruption as a possible mechanism driving our results. Fourth, the program rules set out a transparent and standardized set of grievance redressal rules, laying out clearly the role for local leaders. By leveraging unique administrative data on the universe of grievances filed by households who were not included in the initial list of beneficia- ries, we can examine the precise means through which opposition-aligned politicians are able to add more enrollees to the program. The program implementation process and role of local politicians is detailed in Section 2 .

Using data from administrative records and a close election regression discontinuity design, we document a significant increase in household enrollment in the program in the areas where politicians are from the ruling national party. Aligned politicians tend to enroll 24-28 higher numbers of new households into the program compared to opposition politicians after the election of local politicians, whereas there was no statistically significant differences between two areas before the elections. These new beneficiaries are more likely to complete the housing reconstruction and additional enrollment does not delay the reconstruction process. Such efficiency in reconstruction process is supported by higher number of inspection by engineers and officials in ruling areas.

We demonstrate that program’s grievance redressal system as potential mechanism through which ruling politicians could influence the reconstruction process. The grievance redressal mechanism established formal channels through which disaster- affected households could voice their concerns to the National Reconstruction Au- thority (NRA). This system enabled affected families to contest their exclusion from beneficiary lists when they believed they met eligibility criteria but were overlooked during initial assessments. Additionally, it provided a structured process for house- holds to lodge complaints about various aspects of the reconstruction process, from issues with grant disbursements to document verification. We show ruling areas re- ceive more grievance applications, most likely due to the higher expectation of the populance who may view ruling-party leaders as more responsive or having more au- thority to their concerns, which could motivate them to voice their grievances. Despite higher application lodged, two areas show no difference in acceptance rates, suggesting ruling leaders aren’t displaying favoritism or undue bending of rules. Rather, ruling politicians appear more responsive to public demands.

Moreover, we examine the distributional consequences of the politicians’ actions on program implementation. We find the effect is pronounced in rural and severely im- pacted areas, precisely the locations where earthquake damage, and consequently, the need for reconstruction aid, was higher. Moreover, more enrollment occured in areas were underpriviledged social groups are in minority and in wards where politicians are from underpriviledged group. This may reflect attempts by ruling politicians to show inclusiveness or to bolster support among historically marginalized communities.

We also explore the other potential mechanism of the increased household’s enrollment i.e., politicians might have been engaged in caste-based favouritism. Favouritism in resource allocation is the most common form of political influence. We find that there is no difference in likelihood of so-called privileged-caste groups enrolled into the program compared with the other groups. We also find no evidence of politicians favouring the same-caste beneficiaries.

Next we explored the strategic motive for politicians for electoral benefits through the distribution of public resoruces. Our analysis shows that ruling politicians in disaster-affected areas where the Earthquake Housing Reconstruction Program was implemented received higher vote shares in the next local elections compared to those in non-disaster areas. Despite this electoral advantage, it did not translate into sig- nificantly improved personal reelection outcomes or enhance their party’s likelihood of winning seats.

These results shed light on the often overlooked area of political economy research about distributive politics i.e., potential for local democratic institutions to correct the previously misallocated distribution of public resources, particularly in emergency sit- uations like natural disasters. Past studies document the cases of politicians showing strategic behaviour in allocating public resources for electoral benefits. Several chan- nels exist to exert such influence in allocating public funding. Co-partisanship ( Birch and i Coma, 2023 ; Schneider and Kunze, 2023 ), political connection ( Trinh et al., 2022 ), regional preferences ( Hodler and Raschky, 2014 ) or ethnic connection ( Burgess et al., 2015 ; De Luca et al., 2018 ) can motivate such favouritism and contributes to distributive politics ( de Benedictis-Kessner and Warshaw, 2016 ; Schneider and Kunze, 2023 ). For example, in the US, Schneider and Kunze (2023) record historical political bias in allocating presidential disaster declarations.

The results also add to the literature on the causal link of disasters affecting politics. Disaster affects voter decisions with implications for politicians’ chances for re-election ( Bodet et al., 2016 ; Masiero and Santarossa, 2021 ). Moreover, disasters can have a broader impact on a country’s political system. In a cross-country setting, ( Rahman et al., 2022 ) island countries hit by storms tend to witness democratic backsliding on the back of rulers placating citizens with post-disaster assistance.

The paper also contributes to decentralised governance and its impact on disaster litera- ture. The multi-tiered governance system may harm efficiency and erode accountability by spreading the power and resources to many jurisdictions ( Boffa et al., 2016 ). Federal countries often witness higher levels of government disproportionately allocating public funds to co-partisan local politicians. On the other hand, localised governance can bet- ter respond to the citizens’ needs than distant centralised decision-making units. The latter is true when the electoral are better informed and demand greater accountability ( Besley et al., 2002 ).

The rest of the paper proceeds as follows: Section 2  provides the institutional and con- textual information surrounding the Earthquake Housing Reconstruction Program and local governments in Nepal. Section 3 describes the data sources and set-up empirical approach. Section 4 presents preliminary results, including robustness and placebo tests. Section 5 concludes.

A powerful 7.6-magnitude earthquake struck Nepal on April 25, 2015 3 , affecting eight million people, more than a quarter of the country’s population. The disaster killed around 9,000 people and injured over 22,000 ( NRA, 2021a ). The economic costs of the earthquake is estimated to be USD 7.1 billion, a fifth of the country’s GDP ( NRA, 2021b ). The Government of Nepal (GON) estimates that a million private houses were destroyed, and another three hundred thousand were partially damaged ( NPC, 2015 ). Geographically, the earthquake affected thirty-two of the country’s 77 districts, among which 14 were severely affected, and another 18 neighbouring districts were moderately affected. This covered 2474 wards of 282 municipalities. Top panel in Figure 2 maps the quake intensity across municipalities.

In the aftermath, GON conducted various rescue and relief operations before under- taking largest post-disaster reconstruction initiative in the country’s history. The re- construction program focused on rebuilding the damaged public infrastructure and pri- vate properties. Among them was the Earthquake Housing Reconstruction Program (EHRP) introduced in late 2015, seven months after the earthquake, to aid rebuilding of almost a million private residential houses. EHRP adopted an owners-driven model wherein the program would provide a cash grant to homeowners to reconstruct the damaged houses and homeowners were responsible for construction ( Pokharel et al., 3 It was followed by more than 300 aftershocks greater than magnitude 4.0 within a month, including a major 6.8 magnitude that struck on May 11, 2015, amplifying human casualties and destruction of properties.

2018 ). Each household would receive NPR 300,000 (approximately USD 3,000 at 2015 constant, which equivalents three times the country’s per capita income and covers a third of average housing construction costs) for the fully damaged houses and upto NPR 100,000 for partially damaged houses that requires retrofitting. Households were ex- pected to co-finance the shortfall of reconstruction cost. The grant funds were released in three stages, contingent upon achieving specific reconstruction milestones and sub- sequent verification by qualified engineers for compliance to technical standards. See appendix A.1 for more details on reconstruction program.

The Government of Nepal established the National Reconstruction Authority (NRA) to coordinate reconstruction efforts. Centrally, the NRA operated through a two-tiered governance framework: a steering committee chaired by the Prime Minister and an ex- ecutive committee led by the Prime Minister’s appointee, centralizing decision-making authority and giving the Prime Minister direct influence over reconstruction processes ( Manandhar et al., 2022 ). At local level, it had district-level project implementation units 4 that managed beneficiary enrollment, training, communication, technical as- sistance, social mobilization, and reporting activities. Multilateral agencies like the World Bank 5 supported EHRP with financial and technical assistance.

The program implemented a dual approach to identify beneficiaries for the housing reconstruction program. In the 11 most severely affected districts, they conducted a comprehensive census where every household underwent building damage assessment. For the remaining areas—three districts in Kathmandu Valley and 17 moderately im- pacted districts—they employed a mixed approach with verification, surveying only 4 NRA established central-level project implementation units (CLPIUs) in other ministries too including the Ministry of Urban Development (MOULD), the Ministry of Education, and the Ministry of Culture, Tourism, and Civil Aviation to handle reconstruction of other infrastrucures like road or schools. 5 The World Bank coordinated the Nepal Earthquake Housing Reconstruction Program Multi- Donor Trust Fund (MDTF) that was established to provide reconstruction aid from multilateral and bilateral donor agencies like the Asian Development Bank and USAID, among others. MDTF partially funded the EHRP for constructing 350,000 houses, while the Government of Nepal financed the remaining houses.

those households that self-identified as earthquake victims for damage evaluation ( CBS, 2017 ). This on-site assessment process involved approximately 6,000 trained survey- ors with engineering backgrounds over six-months period. These surveyors collected detailed information at the building, household, and individual levels from 1.2 million households across the affected regions.

The damage assessment categorised the houses into grades 1-5. Grade 5 is defined as the total or near total collapse of the building, and Grade 1 damage is having thin cracks in the plaster (outer layer) of the house that require only architectural repairs 6 Based on the grading, engineers recommended if households are eligible for reconstruction grants, retrofitting assistance or not eligible for any grants. Houses with damage grades 3-5 are eligible for reconstruction grants, whereas those under grade 2 damage would receive retrofitting grants (a third of the full reconstruction money). Households with grade 1 damage would not be eligible for any grant. Figure B.12 shows the distribution of surveyed households by damage level.

Once identified as eligible beneficiary, households could sign participation agreement by submitting the required documentation and receive the first tranche of Rs. 50,000. For the second tranche of Rs. 150,000, recipients needed to complete foundation con- struction up to plinth level, following earthquake safety standards. The final tranche of Rs. 100,000 was released after construction reached roof-band level. Both the second and final payments required physical inspection by engineers and government represen- tatives to verify compliance with technical requirements. If construction failed to meet standards, homeowners needed to make corrections and undergo additional inspections before receiving further funding. Consequently, many households experienced multiple inspection cycles throughout their rebuilding process 7 . The first enrollment occurred 6 The detailed description of grading categories is available in the Table B.8 and the timeline of survey is presented in Table B.9 . 7 Individuals who have initiated reconstruction on their own are eligible for a subsidy, provided that inspections confirm their compliance with technical standards, as well as environmental and social regulations ( NRA, 2021b ).
in June 2016 8 , concurrent with the survey, and continued for several months.

Figure 1: Elections and EHRP grant payments 100K 200K 300K 400K Q12016 Q22016 Q32016 Q42016 Q12017 Q22017 Q32017 Q42017 Q12018 Q22018 Q32018 Q42018 Q12019 Q22019 Q32019 Q42019 Q12020 Q22020 Q32020 Q42020 Q12021 Q22021 Q32021 Q42021 Q12022 Q22022 Q32022 Q42022 No. of grant payment Payment Tranche First Second Third Election Local National Notes: The figure plots the number of grant payments, including first, second and third tranche, over the EHRP period. Two verticle lines indicates timeline for local and national elections in 2017, respectively.

Overall, the program issued 2.3 million grant payments for reconstruction, about two- thirds of which were made after the elected local official took office. Figure 1  shows the timeline of payments following the earthquake and the distribution of three tranches of grants. On the program onset, NRA set the deadline for beneficiary’s enrollment as well as for claiming the second and third tranches. But after repeated procedural delay in conducting surveys, difficulty in verification of documents and also grievances from the people, NRA shifted the deadline several times ( NRA, 2021b ). Ultimately, it adopted the continual enrollment approach, which saw household enrollment throughout the program period. The role of local governments in enrollment of beneficiary is explained in section 2.3 .
These payments were disbursed directly to the households bank account. The program 8 There were some pilot enrollments early Jan 2016.

mandated the distribution of grants through the banking channel to prevent corruption and reduce fiduciary risks. Transparency International Nepal’s investigative report on reconstruction found that payments made through bank accounts not only shortened delivery times but also eliminated malpractices and bribery and contributed to ensuring the proper use of reconstruction support ( Shrestha, 2022 ).

Figure 2B  showcases the spatial distribution of household enrollment across earthquake- affected areas. Most beneficiaries are concentrated in the hardest-hit districts, where earthquake intensity was highest. There is a significant variation in enrollment and program implementation across different regions.

sections.section_name: Local government and election in Nepal
paragraphs: Four months after the earthquake, in September 2015, Nepal adopted a new constitu- tion that federalised the country’s political and administrative structure 9 . Accordingly, 753 local governments (akin to municipalities) and 6743 wards–sub-municipal units– were formed across the country. 10 Following the restructuring, local elections were held in May-June 2017 11 in the midst of reconstruction program which elected more than 36000 local politicians into the office. Although candidates are not required to be political party affiliates by law, Nepal is a multi-party democracy and political landscape is party dominated 12 , and independent candidates are rare. Local politicians are elected for five years and, constitutionally, can be removed only under very stringent conditions 13 . Moreover, the election of each 9 The constitution drafting process started in 2008 with the election of constitution assembly and was ongoing for 7 years as key political actors could not agree on various contentious issues including the form of federalisation. 10 A single municipality can have a minimum of five wards, up to 35 wards in metropolitan areas. The ward executive team includes five people headed by the ward chairman and four members—two reserved for women and Dalit women. So, the executive team at a five-ward municipality would have 27 individuals. This number grows depending on the number of wards. 11 The elections were held in three phases-in May, June and September. The municipalities where third-phase election occurred were unaffected by the earthquake 12 See section A2.4 for description of political landscape and party system in Nepal. 13 Political parties cannot remove them at their discretion.

Figure 2: Earthquake intensity, EHRP beneficiaries and political alignment The map showcases the quake intensity measured in mmi. The red triangle represents the epicentres of the quake in April 15 and May 12. The border is drawn at municipality level. Panel B shows the distribution of beneficiaries across the affected areas.Panel C plots the wards won by ruling party (red) and opposition or non-ruling party (blue). The dark-shaded wards witnessed close-election (based on optimal bandwidth calculation in section 5) and are thus, form the sample for the estimation.

position is independent of one another and are directly elected with first-past-the-post rule, meaning any party with highest number of votes can win a position. Panel B of Figure 2 shows the distribution of the ruling and opposition party politicians across the quake affected areas.

These local elections were held for the first time in 20 years. The previous local election was in 1997 with a five-year mandate. Elections were supposed to be held in 2002 but were delayed due to the ongoing civil war at that time 14 . In the absence of elected political representatives, local administrations were effectively managed by centrally appointed bureaucrats. As a result, local politicians had minimal involvement and influence in the reconstruction process prior to the elections.

National elections: Subsequently, in November-December 2017, Nepal conducted its first national elections under the federal structure established by the new consti- tution. These landmark elections had voters selecting representatives for both federal and provincial assemblies. There are 165 constituency 15 The electoral landscape was primarily dominated by a left alliance between the Communist Party of Nepal (Uni- fied Marxist-Leninist) and the Communist Party of Nepal (Maoist Centre). The left alliance won a decisive victory, securing roughly two-thirds of parliamentary seats, and thus became the ruling coalition parties (ruling party, brevity). Meanwhile, the Nepali Congress, which had previously led the government, was relegated to the role of principal opposition party. While other regional parties gained meaningful repre- sentation, they were primarily concentrated in regions unaffected by the earthquake. Following the elections, the two communist parties formally merged to form the Nepal Communist Party, consolidating their political power. However, this unified party later 14 A local election was held in February 2006 under the direct rule of the monarchy; however, it was boycotted by major political parties and witnessed low voter turnout. The results were later invalidated following the abolition of the monarchy in May 2006. With the start of the peace process, the country established a Constituent Assembly in 2008 tasked with restructuring Nepal’s political and administrative systems, among other objectives. 15 These 165 constituencies are under first-past-the-post voting system. There are 110 proportional representative seats that are allotted based on party’s’ aggregate vote share. There are two provincial constituency for each national constituency.

fractured due to internal disagreements, ultimately leading to the collapse of the rul- ing coalition, leading to the change in government. Our analysis spans this political period, beginning with the 2017 national elections and concluding with the change in government in July 2021.

Within the earthquake-affected areas specifically, the distribution of political power at the local level was concentrated among three major parties, which collectively held 90 percent of ward chair positions. The ruling coalition dominated this local governance landscape, controlling nearly 60 percent of ward chair positions in earthquake-affected areas, while opposition parties held the remaining positions. A single national con- stituency is made-up of several wards spread across many municipalities. Hence, the local politicians are often lower level party cadre and acts are local political mobilizer for the party in the national and provincial elections. Local and national politicians thus share strong hierarchical and informal relationship.

Figure 2C  maps the areas won by ruling and opposition politicians. Again there is spa- tial variation in the wards won by ruling party politicians and those won by opposition. Wards won by the ruling party are shown in red, while those won by the opposition or non-ruling parties are displayed in blue. The dark-shaded wards represent areas where elections were closely contested, as determined using the optimal bandwidth calculation described in section 3 . This selection is methodologically important as it enables more accurate comparisons between areas that share similar characteristics but experienced different electoral outcomes. By focusing on areas with competitive electoral results that could have gone either way, we can better isolate the effects of political alignment while ruling out other potential confounders. We discuss this empirical approach in more detail in section 3 .

Figure A.11 summarises the sequence of significant events following a earthquake in April 2015 prompting immediate rescue and relief operations. During this crisis period, Nepal also underwent a significant political transformation with the promulgation of a new constitution in September 2015, federalising its governance structure. By Decem- ber 2015, the government had created the National Reconstruction Authority (NRA) to coordinate all reconstruction efforts. In early 2016, the NRA initiated comprehensive damage assessment surveys in the most affected districts to identify eligible house- holds for reconstruction assistance, with formal beneficiary enrollment beginning in July 2016. The year 2017 marked a period of simultaneous political development, with the creation of new municipalities and wards in March, followed by thier elections from May to August. The year ended with national and provincial elections in November- December that established a new ruling coalition in the country. Following the election, housing reconstruction efforts accelerated throughout 2018 and 2019, with continued enrollment of beneficiaries and disbursement of grant money. The reconstruction pro- cess formally concluded in December 2021 with the closure of the NRA, transferring remaining responsibilities to relevant agencies and local governments, thus completing a six-year recovery process.

sections.section_name: Why national-local alignment matter for reconstruction?
paragraphs: At national level, NRA, headed by prime minister and run by his appointee, managed the overall reconstruction process, including all key policy and financial decisions. Be- fore election, NRA had district offices for overseeing the program implementation from identifying identification, fund disbursement to technical supervision, and progress re- porting. After the election, the NRA gradually transferred reconstruction program responsibilities and authority to municipalities 16 .

Initially, local government’s responsibility was limited to handling complaints and grievances registered by the people claiming to be beneficiaries–formally, grievance redressal process ( Pokharel et al., 2018 ). Most significant of these changes was the for- mation of grievance redressal committees at the ward, municipal, and federal levels, to 16 Initially, NRA entered into agreements with all municipalities in earthquake-affected districts to transfer responsibilities for post-earthquake reconstruction efforts. Later, procedural by-law was amended to formally make municipalities responsible for the reconstruction activities ( NRA, 2021b ).

handle complaints and grievances from the public. Figure  B.13  showcase the grievance redressal structure and nature of complaints handled at each level. A three-member Ward Grievance Committee, consisted of the ward chairperson, an engineer, and the ward secretary, were formed to handle grievances and coordinate the housing recon- struction program ( NRA, 2021a ). Grievances that can be addressed at the ward or municipality level will be handled locally. Any grievances that cannot be resolved lo- cally must be escalated to the appropriate higher-level Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) committee. This escalation pathway applies to cases where complaints are made against NRA staff or when complainants wish to remain anonymous. The sys- tem is designed with grievances flowing upward through the hierarchy while resolutions flow downward.

The process typically began with households submitting documentation supporting their claim for beneficiary status—evidence of property ownership, proof of earthquake damage, household particulars, and other relevant information. Local officials would then conduct initial verification of these claims. Since ward chairs were the primary interface with beneficiaries, they played key role in receiving the grievance applica- tions from households and potential beneficiaries. Their role involved receiving formal complaints from residents who claimed they should have been included as beneficia- ries but were overlooked during initial assessments. Local officials would collect these grievances, document the claims, verify basic information, and assess the beneficiary’s status as potential before transmitting them up through official channels. Local politi- cians therefore became instrumental in identifying such households for resurvey and new damage assessment.

The grievances stem from myriad of potential issues, including the exclusion of certain households from the survey or incomplete records, or the absence of necessary doc- umentation, among others. These grievances therefore needed to be addressed case- by-case basis and may involve discretion. Additionally, for the households omitted in the previous survey or potentially inaccurate damage assessments were re-surveyed and re-verified. The reverification and resurvey were initiated in response to registered grievances. Reverification aims to determine the accuracy of beneficiary data or rectify errors identified during initial data collection. It also involved verifying the complete- ness and correctness of the information or documentation submitted by the beneficia- ries. In contrast, resurvey efforts focused on addressing complaints from households that believed they were wrongly excluded from assistance programs. Resurveys were conducted for households that were either not surveyed during the initial assessment or were previously deemed ineligible due to damage assessments that did not meet the required threshold for beneficiary status. Final resolution involves households added to the list of beneficiaries or evaluated as non-beneficiaries.

This grievance redressal function served several purposes. First, it provided an acces- sible local point of contact for affected citizens who might otherwise struggle to reach district or national offices. Second, it leveraged local officials’ knowledge of community circumstances to perform preliminary verification. Third, it established a systematic pathway for addressing potential errors or omissions in the beneficiary identification process.

This function established local governments as intermediaries between citizens and na- tional reconstruction authorities and created the foundation for their later expanded role in the reconstruction process. Gradually, local governments’ reconstruction man- date eventually grew to include agreement processing, technical supervision, and imple- mentation oversight. Beneficiaries could then sign agreements at ward offices instead at district headquarter. Additionally, NRA-deployed engineers are now assigned through municipalities, which can track their attendance, mobilize them in the field as required, and even recommend actions against technicians who do not perform as agreed. This shift aims to empower municipalities to address problems independently and increase their effectiveness ( NRA, 2021b ; Shrestha, 2022 ).
On the other hand, national government (through NRA) also play role in the process.

While local politicians can identify new households for enrollment, the final decision would be taken up centrally. NRA also had a set of procedures to handle the grievance, with final decisions made by the steering committee headed by the prime minister ( Joshi, 2019 ; Manandhar et al., 2022 ). With all the recommendation of beneficiary received from the local level, the National Reconstruction Authority (NRA) undertook a re-survey and re-verification process to ensure the inclusion of all genuinely affected households in the reconstruction process. This process resulted in the enrollment of additional beneficiaries into the program. The surveys were conducted after the election of representatives to local governments. Engineers from the municipalities carried out the damage reassessment, while local politicians reviewed these assessments and made recommendations for the inclusion of new beneficiaries. This approach allowed local politicians to exert influence on the reconstruction process, facilitating the enrollment of additional households into the program. Hence, the political linkage to the national government remains crucial. Although all local political office-bearers have the same powers by law, the ruling party politicians have the edge in garnering more resources from central governments.

This study combines electoral data with a comprehensive household-level administra- tive records on housing reconstruction grants. Electoral data provides our analysis’s main running and treatment variables, whereas housing reconstruction grant data pro- vides a key outcome variables of interest.

Reconstruction Data The earthquake housing reconstruction program data is sourced from the EHRP Management Information System (EHRPMIS), which is main- tained by the National Reconstruction Authority. to monitor both physical and fi- nancial progress of housing reconstruction initiatives. We request for and received limited-period read-only access to the dataset from NRA. 17 The MIS system features dedicated modules for initial damage assessment survey docu- mentation, beneficiary enrollment, payment processing, grievance resolution, construc- tion inspection and training administration. Key information captured within this portal include detailed beneficiary demographic profiles, enrollment and geospatial in- formation, reconstruction inspection details, and grant disbursement records.

EHRPMIS records a total of 866,160 beneficiaries identified for the EHRP grant, through the study period until mid-2021. Of these, 829,667 beneficiaries, or 95.78 per- cent, have signed a participation agreement to receive the grant of Rs. 300,000 ( $ 3,000) to rebuild their houses.. Among those who have signed, 99.89 percent have already received the first tranche of Rs. 50,000, while 90.45 percent have received the second tranche of Rs. 150,000. Additionally, 84.77 percent of beneficiaries have received the third and final tranche of Rs. 100,000. Altogether, 2.3 million grant tranches has been paid by the program. During this period, 615,924 beneficiaries completed construction. Additionally, 47,827 beneficiaries were identified to receive the government grant of Rs. 100,000 ( $ 1,000) to retrofit their houses. Of these, 35,027 beneficiaries had received the first tranche, and 2,035 had received the second tranche.

Additionally, the EHRPMIS grievance management module captures comprehensive data on beneficiary complaints registration and redressal outcome. It documents de- tailed records of grievance causes—including exclusion from initial beneficiary lists, disputes over damage assessment classifications, mistake in documentations, and other concerns regarding eligibility. The system also records types of evaluation approach- reverification or resurvey-and final determinations that typically categorize outcomes as upheld assessments, reclassified damage levels, newly qualified benefits, or con- firmed ineligibility. In total, approximately 635,000 grievances were registered, and all 17 The NRA Act included a sunset clause, which led to the Authority winding up after five years, in 2021. Following this, the MIS systems were transferred to the National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Authority (NDRRMA).

were addressed, throughout the reconstruction period. The redressal process included multiple rounds of reviews and verifications of the application and also re-survey of the damaged houses. Subsequently, 27.4% grievance applications were successful and households were added as beneficiary.

Geographically, the beneficiaries spans 2400 wards which spans 32 earthquake affected districts. We aggregate all beneficiary data and other reconstruction outcomes at the ward level, which serves as our primary unit of analysis.

Election Data The electoral data come from the Election Commission of Nepal (ECN), which publishes detailed results of the elections at all levels at the candidates’ level for every position. The data include the candidate’s name, gender, age, party affiliation, position-ran and votes received. Local elections in Nepal follows first-past- the-post methods. That means candidate amassing highest share of votes would win and elected to the position. In 2017, local elections occurred for all 753 municipalities in three phases, each in May, June, and September. Altogether, 148,362 candidates affiliated to 61 political parties or independents ran for the 37,248 distinct positions. Among them, 412 different positions were won unopposed. For this study, the main unit of analysis is the ward, which is administratively below municipalities. Prelimi- nary analysis focuses on the positions of ward chair. Similar data for 2022 election is also available.

Others Data To complement the two main dataset, we use ward-level demographic and earthquake-related characteristics in our empirical estimation. We include ward- level demographic characteristics, like population, areas etc. obtained from the Pop- ulation and Housing Census 2011. These pre-earthquake demographic data serve as important control variables in our analytical framework. Further, we employ data from the United States Geological Survey (USGS) to obtain an exogenous measure of earth- quake intensity: the Modified Mercalli Intensity (MMI). Specifically, we use USGS’s spatially continuous MMI map for the April 2015 earthquake to calculate the mean MMI of each ward by averaging the intensity values across the entire ward polygon.

We use a close election regression discontinuity (RD) design to examine how politi- cians behave when given the opportunity to influence the reconstruction process and outcomes. Specifically, we compare the enrollment of additional households into post- disaster housing reconstruction programs by local politicians (ward chairs) aligned with the national ruling party versus those belonging to opposition parties. However, wards won by the ruling party might be systematically different from those from the non- ruling party on various observed and unobserved characteristics that might impact the outcome. As a result, a simple comparison between the two groups of politicians would not provide a causal interpretation.

This paper addresses the issue by focusing on ward chairs who belong to the same ruling coalition party as the national government (referred to hereafter as the ruling party) as described in section 2.2 . It compares wards where the ruling party’s ward chair narrowly won to those areas where the ruling party’s candidate barely lost the election. The key identifying assumption is that, at a sufficiently narrow margin of victory, areas that were narrowly won by politicians aligned with the ruling party are similar to those that were narrowly lost to the opposition. Formally, this implies that observed and unobserved characteristics—excluding the outcome variables—do not change discontinuously at the margin between winning and losing.

We use the specification 1 with local linear regression within a given bandwidth on either side of the treatment cutoff to identify the effect of political alignment disaster aid allocation. Since it is the first-past-the-post election, the winning cutoff is zero in this setting.

Y w =  β 0  +  β 1 A v  ×  f ( margin w ) +  X w  +  ϵ w (1) Y w is the outcome of interest at ward w . The key outcome variables of interest include the number of households enrolled in the reconstruction program following local gov- ernment elections, the number and total amount of grant payments received, and the duration of the housing reconstruction cycle—measured as the number of days between the signing of the grant agreement and the disbursement of the final grant tranches. In addition to these primary outcomes, we apply the same empirical framework to explore potential mechanisms, such as the grievance redressal systems, and to conduct heterogeneity analyses across relevant subgroups.

A w is a binary treatment variable indicating whether the elected ward chair in the ward w is aligned with the national ruling party. margin w is a running variable that measures the margin of victory in the local election for the ward chair position, and f ( · ) is a polynomial function calculated based on the margin of victory. X w is the ward-level control, including the population, area, and earthquake intensity measured by avearge the modified mercalli intensity to proxy for damage level. The control also includes the number of parties contested and the winning party’s vote share in the 2017 local election. All controls are measured at the baseline unless otherwise stated. Finally, ϵ w is the error term.

Running Variable The running variable is the margin of victory( margin ) of ward- chair position in local elections 2017. The margin of victory is the difference in the votes received by the two top-most candidates from the ruling party and the non-ruling party. Since a coalition of two national parties was in power, they are grouped as ruling parties, whereas all other parties are categorised as non-ruling parties. See section 2.2 for detailed discussion about ruling parties or coalition. For each ward constituency w , if v tot represents the total number of valid votes received by all candidates, v win the votes for the candidate aligned with the national coalition and v loss the votes for the non-aligned candidate, then we can define running variable as:

margin w = v win – v loss v tot This results in the winning margin of victory being zero 18 . Accordingly, if the winner is from the ruling party, a treatment value of 1 is assigned and zero otherwise. There- fore, by construction, each ward’s winning margin would remain between 100 and 100 percent. The margin w would be positive if the aligned candidate wins in ward w and negative when they lose. Consequently, when the margin exceeds zero (i.e., positive margin), the ruling party wins and gets the treatment value ( A w ) of 1 and otherwise zero.

3.2.1 Testing Identifying Assumptions Manipulation (Density) Test The large number of parties vying for any position means a narrow margin wins many positions. It is also indicative of satisfying the critical assumption of the RD design that the running variable, ruling party preference, is continuous across the threshold. Figure A.8 plots the running variable margin, i.e., the ruling party’s margin of victory for the entire range in the bin of 1 percent. It shows continuity below and above the threshold and no apparent sorting or jumps around it. Figure 3B shows the density test following Cattaneo and Titiunik, 2022 . The density test checks the existence of systematic manipulation by testing if the density of units is continuous around the zero (cut-off point) value.

The large number of parties vying for any position means many positions are won by a narrow margin. It is also indicative of satisfying the key assumption of the RD design that the running variable, ruling party preference, is continuous across the threshold. Figure  A.8a  plots the running variable  margin i.e., the ruling party’s margin of victory for the entire range in the bin of 1 percent. It shows continuity below and above the threshold and no obvious sorting or jumps around it. Figure A.8b shows the density test following Cattaneo and Titiunik, 2022 .

Balance Test Further, regression discontinuity estimates can only be interpreted 18 On the rare occasions when the top two candidates receive an equal number of votes, resulting in a zero vote margin, a coin toss is used to determine the winner.

causally if the baseline covariates are balanced across the threshold. In other words, other ward level observables should not jump discontinuously at the cut-off. Figure  A.9 shows the graphical evidence of the balance of the same key baseline characteristics. Each panel plots the ruling party’s margin of victory on the x-axis and respective base- line characteristics on the y-axis. As we can observe, the ruling and non-ruling wards are very similar in population and area size, both of which impact the damage. The vote share of the winning candidate at the margin also balances well. Importantly, there is no difference in households’ enrolment before the election. Table 1: Summary statistics Opposition Wards (N = 701) Ruling Wards (N = 892) Variable Mean SD Mean SD Diff. in means Outcomes Beneficiary Enrollment Before (Count) 238.2 289.5 235.1 283.0 -3.1 Beneficiary Enrollment After (Count) 66.6 86.0 72.6 94.1 6.0 Reconstruction Duration Before (Days) 830.0 174.5 835.5 163.2 5.5 Reconstruction Duration After (Days) 521.9 189.2 507.9 185.6 -14.0 Covariates Earthquake Intensity (mmi) 6.8 0.9 6.7 0.9 -0.1 Population (2011) 4043.1 5309.9 4433.5 5878.0 390.4 Area (sq. km) 18.6 31.5 18.3 42.8 -0.3 Number of Parties (2017 Elections) 3.6 0.6 3.7 0.5 0.1 Vote Share (2017 Elections) 50.3 9.8 49.4 9.4 -0.8 Sample Wards N % N % Rural 484 69.0 573 64.2 Urban 217 31.0 319 35.8 Notes: The terms ‘Before’ and ‘After’ in the outcome variables refer to the periods before and after the local government took over the reconstruction process, with ’Before’ representing the time prior to the election of the national ruling government in December 2017, and ’After’ representing the period following that election.

Augmenting the balance evidence, Table 1 presents descriptive statistics for the out- come, explanatory variables, and covariates. The mean-difference column showed no significant difference between the control and treated wards. Corroborating that result, we also find no discontinuous changes at the thresholds established by RD estimates. The density and balance test supports the identification design proposed. It gives cre- dence that the estimates can identify the causal relations between political alignment and post-disaster reconstruction outcomes.

We present our results and discuss them in four stages. First, we examine the impact of political alignment on EHRP program implementation patterns and key reconstruc- tion outputs, exploring how local political affiliation with ruling national coalition influenced the pace and effectiveness of recovery efforts. Second, we investigate the grievance redressal management system as a potential causal mechanism connecting political alignment of local officials to reconstruction outcomes. Next, we assess the distributional implications of political behavior, evaluating equity concerns in resource allocation across affected communities. Finally, we examine the strategic electoral mo- tives of local politicians and the electoral consequences of their actions in influencing the disaster recovery program.

sections.section_name: Effect on program implementation and outcomes
paragraphs: The previous section demonstrated that there is no statistically significant difference in household enrollment patterns and other characteristics between areas governed by ruling-party and opposition-party politicians. Therefore, the regression discontinuity estimates presented offer causal evidence of this relationship. We present our results for two distinct periods separated by the formation of a ruling coalition following the Nov- Dec 2017 national elections. The first period, from the beginning of the reconstruction program until the election, is referred to as the ”Before election” period. The second period, from after the election until the end of the national coalition government, is designated as the ”After election” period. It was during this latter period that the national-local political alignment took effect and influenced the reconstruction process. We include results from the ”Before election” period for comparison purposes.

We begin by presenting graphical evidence of treatment effects on key reconstruction related outcomes, specifically the enrollment of new beneficiaries into the program. Figure 3 illustrates the differential impact on additional beneficiary enrollments in the post-earthquake housing reconstruction program both before (Panel A) and after (Panel B) the election of local politicians. We can observe the clear discontinuity or ‘the jump’ at the cut-off which illustrates narrowly elected ruling-party officials influenced the reconstruction process by enrolling additional beneficiaries, in contrast to similarly situated opposition politicians. Notably, we see no such discontinuity or differences in beneficiary enrollment pattern in the same areas before the elections. The combined result provides strong causal evidence that ruling-party aligned local politicians, particurlarly those who marginally won elections, tend to provide more disaster aid to beneficiary, relative to opposition aligned politicians. Figure 3: Political Alignment and Household Enrollment −80 −40 0 −10 −5 0 Winning Vote Margin Additional HH Enrollment (a) Before Elections −20 0 −10 −5 0 Winning Vote Margin Additional HH Enrollment (b) After Elections Notes: This figure shows residualized household enrollment into the EHRP program before (Panel A) and after (Panel B) the election of local politicians in 2017. The horizontal axis represents the winning vote margin for the ward chair position. A positive margin indicates a victory by a candidate aligned with the ruling national party, while a negative margin reflects a win by an opposition candidate. Each point represents the conditional mean of the outcome, estimated using a triangular kernel with MSE optimal bandwidth. Standard errors are clustered at the ward level.

Table 2 presents the precise regression discontinuity (RD) estimates of the effect of political alignment on program enrollment. Columns 1–2 report the impact of having a ruling party-affiliated politician in charge of a ward on the number of new household enrollments after the local election, while columns 3–4 show the estimates for the pre- election period. The results suggest that in wards represented by politicians aligned with the national ruling party, approximately 24-28 more beneficiaries enrolled in the reconstruction program compared to wards with non-aligned politicians. This consti- tute 31-36 percent higher than mean enrollment across all wards after the elections.

Table 2: Effect of political alignment on beneficiary enrollment Number of beneficiary enrollment After election Before election (1) (2) (3) (4) Ruling politician 23.59** 27.76** 15.55 -5.23 (9.66) (11.31) (33.81) (48.52) P-value 0.014 0.020 0.661 0.891 Bandwidth 10.61 17.35 10.61 10.61 Observations 692 993 740 740 Polynomial Linear Quadratic Linear Quadratic Table presents the regression discontinuity estimates of impact of having local politician aligned with rul- ing national party on the enrollment of additional households in post-earthquake reconstruction program. Columns 1 - 2 reports the impact after the local politician assume office post-2017-elections and columns 3 - 4 shows the effect in same areas before elections. The coefficient ”Ruling politician” indicates impact in areas where ward-chairperson narrowly won compared to the areas where they narrowly lost. The band- width is selected optimally using mean-square error (MSE) method and all estimates use triangular kernel. All estimation includes covariates include ward-level population (log), area (log), number of parties with candidates for ward-chairperson position in local elections, vote share of elected candidate and earthquake intensity measured by modified mercalli intensity (MMI). The estimation after the elections also controls for baseline enrollment numbers. The coefficients are estimated using local linear regression (columns 1, and 3) and quadratic (columns 2 and 4). Robust standard errors are in parentheses. Significance levels: *** p <  0 . 01, ** p <  0 . 05, * p <  0 . 1.

The observed effect remains robust across various bandwidth specifications, selections methods and polynomial order. The robustness of results are shown in Table  A.7  which shows the effect remains generally stable across different specification choices—whether using half or double the MSE-optimal bandwidth—and when higher-order polynomials like cubic and quartic terms are applied. Moreover, the estimates hold up across alternative bandwidth selection methods and kernel functions. Overall, the consistency of the results across these various specifications strengthens the credibility of the main finding: political alignment appears to play a meaningful role in shaping program enrollment after local politicians took office.

Similarly, Figure  4  demonstrates that the estimated treatment effects remain consistent across a wide range of bandwidth choices. Here, bandwidth refers to the winning vote margin around the cutoff of zero that is used to compute the local estimate of the treatment effect. A smaller bandwidth restricts the analysis to observations very close to the cutoff—that is, areas where elections were decided by a narrow vote margin. Conversely, as we move to larger bandwidths, more areas are included in the analysis, encompassing elections decided by increasingly larger margins. The fact that the figure shows stable and consistent estimates even when the bandwidth deviates from this optimal point enhances the robustness of our findings. It suggests that the estimated effects are not overly sensitive to the choice of bandwidth, thereby reinforcing confidence in the reliability of the results.
Figure 4: RD Estimates across different bandwidths 0 70 Bandwidth Beneficiary enrollment number Notes: This figure plots RD estimates across the bandwidth.

Interestingly, Figure 4 also depicts a compelling pattern regarding the relationship be- tween electoral margins and politician performance. Ruling politicians who narrowly won elections demonstrate significantly higher effects on reconstruction outcomes, with these effects diminishing as victory margins increase. This pattern is intuitively consis- tent with theoretical expectations about political incentives. Politicians who secured their positions by narrow margins appear to exhibit greater motivation to deliver tan- gible results, presumably due to perceived electoral vulnerability. In contrast, those who won with “safe” margin demonstrate reduced performance incentives, suggesting a form of complacency that stems from electoral security. The heightened performance among narrowly-elected politicians likely reflects strategic behavior aimed at consol- idating their tenuous political positions and building stronger electoral prospects for future contests. We acknowledge that using a wider bandwidth increases the number of observations, which can help reduce variance but may also introduce bias by including data points farther from the cutoff—potentially drawing in areas that are less com- parable. Nonetheless, this finding contributes to our understanding of how electoral competition intensity shapes politician responsiveness and service delivery, particularly in post-disaster reconstruction contexts.

The influence of ruling party politicians extends beyond the identification and enroll- ment of additional beneficiaries into the other important aspects of housing reconstruc- tion program to encompass a broader range of implementation processes and outcomes. In this section, we examine how political alignment affects various aspects of the re- construction program. To recall from section 2.1 , the grant was disbursed in three tranches, each tied to specific reconstruction milestones. The first tranche was released upon the signing of the participation agreement. To receive the second tranche, ben- eficiaries were required to complete construction of foundation up to the plinth level, in accordance with earthquake-resistant building codes. The third and final tranche was disbursed once the construction up to roof-band level was completed. The sec- ond and third tranches were subject to on-site inspection and verification by engineers and government officials to ensure compliance with technical standards. If the recon- struction work does not meet the required technical standards, households are asked to make the necessary adjustments and are subject to additional inspections before the next tranche is released. As a result, many households undergo multiple rounds of inspections throughout the reconstruction process.

It is important to note that not all beneficiaries who enroll in the program ultimately complete the reconstruction. According to data from the National Reconstruction Au- thority (NRA), around 15 percent of households who signed the participation agree- ment and received the first tranche did not go on to receive the third and final tranche. These cases are classified by the program as incomplete reconstructions.

Another important aspect of the reconstruction program was the speed at which ben- eficiaries were able to rebuild their damaged homes. Accelerating reconstruction was one of the program’s key policy objectives. However, the dataset does not contain in- formation on the exact dates when households completed their reconstruction or how long the rebuilding process actually took. To address this, we approximate the re- construction duration by calculating the time between the release of the first tranche and the disbursement of the final tranche. We refer to this as the completion time, measured in days.

Table 3 Panels A-C analyze how ruling politicians affected different payment mile- stone achievements in the reconstruction process. We observe number of beneficiaries receiving only first tranche increased after elections while there are no difference be- tween ruling and opposition ruled areas before the election. Similarly, for households receiving both first and second tranches, the effect becomes significant only after the election. Most notably, for completed households (receiving all tranches), we observe a substantial effect after the election, suggesting ruling politicians not only enrolled higher number of new beneficiaries but also accelerated project completion rates i.e., ensured households complete the reconstruction of houses. Additionally, this does not comes at the costs of longer reconstruction period, though. Panel D examines comple- tion time in days, showing that while ruling areas experienced longer reconstruction periods both before and after the election, these differences are not statistically signif- icant. This indicates that while ruling politicians enrolled more beneficiaries, that did not alter the pace of reconstruction once households were enrolled.

Table 3: Effect of political alignment on various reconstruction outcomes (A) (B) (C) First tranche only First + Second tranche All tranches (Completed HH) HH enrolled: Before After Before After Before After Ruling politician 1.57 9.46** 0.36 3.46** 6.11 12.65** (1.97) (4.28) (0.44) (1.41) (8.69) (6.40) P-value 0.336 0.027 0.364 0.018 0.306 0.037 Bandwidth 10.61 10.61 10.61 10.61 10.61 10.61 Observations 621 692 621 692 621 692 (D) (E) (F) Completion time (days) Total grant (NPR million) Inspection frequency HH enrolled: Before After Before After Before After Ruling politician 27.58 7.79 -4.70 3.75** 20.92 40.70** (23.44) (28.12) (3.63) (1.92) (18.51) (17.93) P-value 0.208 0.816 0.149 0.039 0.173 0.024 Bandwidth 10.61 10.61 10.61 10.61 10.61 10.61 Observations 737 665 443 692 621 692 Notes: Table presents the regression discontinuity estimates of the impact of having a local politician aligned with the opposition party at the national level on various post-earthquake reconstruction outcomes. Panel A shows the number of beneficiaries who received the first tranche of cash grant, Panel B shows those who received both first and second tranches, and Panel C presents households that received all tranches upon completing house reconstruction. “Before” and “After” refer to households enrolled before and after the local elections. Panel D reports the effect on average duration (days) to complete reconstruction. Panel E provides total grant amounts aggregated at the ward level. Panel F captures the frequency of insepections by engineers and officials, which is required for households to receive additional tranche. All estimates use a uniform kernel, linear regression, and includes covariates. Robust standard errors in parentheses. Significance levels: *** p <  0 . 01, ** p <  0 . 05, * p <  0 . 1.

The differential performance between ruling and opposition politicians manifests in tangible financial implications for their respective constituencies. Panel E quantifies these financial consequences, revealing a marked shift in resource allocation patterns following the election. While pre-election data shows no statistically significant dif- ference in total grant disbursement across politically aligned areas, the post-election period exhibits a substantial divergence. ruling-aligned wards received significantly greater financial resources (3.75 million more) compared to opposition wards. This asymmetric flow of reconstruction funds directly corresponds with our primary finding that ruling politicians enabled a greater number of beneficiary enrollments and project completions. Panel F shows that ruling politicians could achieve these results through more frequent inspections of the reconstruction process. The observed reconstruction disparities under ruling-party officials may be attributed to the frequency and pri- oritization of construction inspections. This procedural mechanism holds particular significance within the reconstruction process, as the disbursement of subsequent grant tranches to beneficiary households is contingent upon favorable inspection outcomes by authorized officials and engineers. By potentially expediting inspections in their constituencies, ruling politicians could accelerate the reconstruction cycle, enabling their constituents to access funding tranches more rapidly than those in opposition- controlled areas. This inspection-based procedural advantage represents a plausible administrative channel through which political alignment translates into tangible re- construction outcomes, as timely inspections directly determine a household’s ability to progress through the multi-stage grant disbursement process.

Collectively, these regression discontinuity estimates demonstrate that ruling politi- cians’ influence manifested primarily after the election and extended beyond mere enrollment to include successful milestone achievement, increased funding allocation, and more effective bureaucratic processing. These results suggest opposition politicians leveraged their positions to accelerate reconstruction outcomes.

In this section, we examine how multilevel political alignments affect reconstruction outcomes. Specifically, we investigate whether the influence of ruling-party ward chairs on reconstruction processes is amplified by alignment with higher-level political actors: municipal mayors and constituency-level national politicians. This analysis explores how vertical political coordination across different governance tiers might enhance ward chairs’ ability to deliver reconstruction benefits.

The first dimension examines mayoral alignment at the local level, specifically whether the ward chair and municipal mayor share political party affiliation (aligned mayor) or represent opposing parties (non-aligned mayor). This relationship is particularly significant given that wards function as administrative sub-units within municipalities. When ward chairs and mayors belong to the same political party, their aligned interests may facilitate coordinated administrative decision-making and strategic resource allo- cation throughout the reconstruction process, potentially enhancing implementation efficiency.

Second, we investigate how national-level political dynamics affect local reconstruction implementation by measuring the electoral security of constituency representatives. We categorize national politicians’ electoral performance into wide margins (above the national median) and narrow margins (below the national median). These national representatives potentially function as intermediaries connecting ward chairs to central government resources and policy support. Their electoral security may also shape reconstruction implementation incentives—either to shore up support in competitive districts or reward loyalty in partisan strongholds. Table 4 presents the results of such conditions on beneficiary enrollments.

The top panel presents the enrollment effects along two dimensions separately, whereas the bottom panel presents the nested estimation. The table demonstrates that ben- eficiary enrollment significantly increases in areas where mayors are not aligned with the ruling party. This effect is particularly pronounced in constituencies with wide na- tional election margins, suggesting strategic allocation of program resources. Notably, in wards where mayors are aligned with the ruling party and the national politician secured a wide margin of victory, enrollment rises by 47.11 households. Conversely, in politically competitive areas—characterized by narrow victory margins—where the mayor is from an opposition party, enrollment increases by 51.08 households. These patterns point toward political patronage, wherein ruling party politicians appear to reward loyal constituencies with aligned local leadership, while also channeling benefits to opposition-led areas in competitive districts to bolster electoral support.

Table 4: Political multi-alignment and beneficiary enrollment Mayor alignment National election margin Not-aligned Aligned Wide Narrow Ruling politician 41.90** 10.07 32.51** 10.55 (20.28) (14.69) (13.27) (16.21) P-value 0.048 0.455 0.015 0.516 Bandwidth 10.61 10.61 10.61 10.61 Observations 312 420 350 364 National election margin Wide Narrow Mayor Not-aligned Mayor Aligned Mayor Not-aligned Mayor Aligned Ruling politician 13.68 47.11*** 51.08* -12.58 (25.06) (12.86) (27.28) (21.87) P-value 0.583 0.000 0.084 0.637 Bandwidth 10.61 10.61 10.61 10.61 Observations 140 210 166 198 The table presents presents heterogeneity in enrollment effect based on political characteristics. Mayor alignment is defined at the municipal level, where a municipality is considered aligned if the ward chairman and mayor belong to the same party and not aligned if they are from different parties. National election margin classifies wards within a constituency as narrowly contested if the margin of victory is below the sample median and safe if the margin is above the median. Top panel presents effect margin is above the median. All estimates are based on a consistent bandwidth selected using the mean-square error (MSE) method in our base model. All estimates use uniform kernel and linear regression estimation. Robust standard errors are in parentheses. Significance levels: *** p < 0 . 01, ** p < 0 . 05, * p <  0 . 1.

National Reconstruction Authority (NRA) established a Grievance Redressal Mech- anism (GRM) to address complaints related to the housing reconstruction program. The GRM was designed as a three-tiered system, operating at the ward, municipal, and central levels, to ensure that grievances were handled efficiently and fairly. At the ward and municipal levels, local grievance committees were formed, each comprising three members and led by the respective ward or municipal chief. These committees were responsible for resolving local issues such as disputes over ownership, corrections in beneficiary details, and verification of household eligibility. Their authority was limited to matters within their administrative boundaries and aimed to provide quick and accessible redressal for affected residents. At the central level, more complex grievances were reviewed by a designated member of the NRA’s executive committee. This included cases involving individuals who had been mistakenly left out of the ben- eficiary list, those seeking eligibility for retrofitting or new construction support, and complaints about properties that were missed during the initial damage assessments. There were also grievances concerning the type of support received—some households contested their eligibility for retrofitting grants instead of full reconstruction assistance, and vice versa. However, complaints related to house design, construction materials, or proposed resettlement plans were rarely reviewed or addressed through the grievance process 19 .

Prior to the delegation of authority to ward and municipal grievance management committees following the 2017 elections, the centralized nature of the grievance review and redressal system created significant procedural delays. This inefficient structure resulted in extended resolution timeframes, generating considerable anxiety and con- fusion among affected individuals awaiting determinations regarding their cases. The inability to address concerns at local levels contributed to a backlog of unresolved grievances, until the local politician took office.

Once a complaint is registered, local officials at the ward level are the first to review the application. The grievance redressal process involves evaluating whether the com- plainant qualifies as a genuine beneficiary. This typically includes either conducting a resurvey—if the household was omitted from the initial assessment—or re-verifying the reported damage or any missing documentation. Through this resurvey and rever- ification process, beneficiary selection became more flexible and allowed local political leaders to gain discretionary influence in identifying and approving beneficiaries for the reconstruction program.

In this section, we examine whether political alignment influences grievance behavior among the population. We focus on three key indicators related to the grievance redressal process. First, we consider the number of grievance applications registered, 19 However, not all grievances can be addressed at the ward level. To ensure accountability, an appeals mechanism allows complainants to escalate unresolved cases to higher authorities, such as central-level NRA offices, for further review.

which reflects citizens’ demand for reconstruction assistance and their willingness to engage with the system. Second, we analyze the number of applications that were successfully resolved—specifically, those in which applicants were recognized as eligible beneficiaries. Third, we assess the success rate, measured as the proportion of successful applications relative to the total number of grievances filed. These indicators together help us understand both the responsiveness of the system and the potential influence of political factors on grievance outcomes. Table 5 presents the result of this assessment.

Table 5: Effect on grievance registration and success rate Panel A: Number of grievance registered All Rural Urban Ruling politician 59.66** 104.67*** -58.66 (27.92) (39.67) (47.07) P-value 0.017 0.004 0.298 Panel B: Number of grievance beneficiary All Rural Urban Ruling politician 14.33** 24.36** -30.80 (7.55) (12.50) (36.29) P-value 0.043 0.041 0.512 Panel C: Grievance success rate (%) All Rural Urban Ruling politician 0.02 0.03 0.01 (0.03) (0.03) (0.05) P-value 0.516 0.339 0.719 Bandwidth 10.61 10.61 10.61 Observations 719 492 261 Table reports the regression discontinuity estimates of impact of having local politician aligned with ruling national party on the enrollment of additional households on reconstruction-related grievance redressal procedure and outcomes, across the sample area including rural and urban sub-areas. The key indicators include number of grievance application registered (Panel A); number of successful grievance applicants who become beneficiaries, and the grievance success rate, a share of successful grievance application to total number of grievance applications (Panel C). All estimates are based on the consistent bandwidth of 10.61 which was selected optimally using mean-square error (MSE) method in our base model (Column 1, Table 2). All estimates use uniform kernel and linear regression estimation. The covariates including area, population, vote share, number of parties and earthquake intensity. Robust standard errors are in parentheses. Significance levels: *** p <  0 . 01, ** p <  0 . 05, * p <  0 . 1.

Panel A presents evidence that the number of grievance applications registered is higher in areas represented by ruling-party politicians. This pattern suggests that citizens may perceive ruling-party leaders as more capable or more willing to act on their concerns, which could encourage them to come forward with their grievances. Even though opposition politicians have the same procedural authority to process grievances, the higher volume of applications in ruling-party areas may reflect a perception of greater access to resources or political influence. In this sense, the public may believe that aligning their claims with ruling politicians increases the chances of being heard and supported in the reconstruction process.

Panel B focuses on the number of successful grievance applications—those in which claimants were ultimately enrolled as program beneficiaries. The results show that this number is also higher in ruling-party areas, reinforcing the idea that ruling politicians are more responsive to constituents’ demands. This could be due to a combination of factors, such as better coordination with bureaucratic structures, stronger political incentives to deliver visible results, or simply a greater volume of grievances to act upon. Regardless of the cause, the outcome suggests that more people in ruling-party areas benefit from the grievance mechanism.

Panel C, however, reveals a more nuanced picture. When looking at the grievance application success rate—that is, the share of total applications that resulted in bene- ficiary enrollment—there is little difference between ruling and opposition areas. This implies that while ruling politicians may facilitate a greater number of grievance filings and subsequent enrollments, the rate at which applications are deemed valid and ap- proved remains consistent across political alignments. In other words, both ruling and opposition politicians appear to follow the same standards in evaluating and resolving grievances. This consistency suggests adherence to formal procedures and offers no indication of systematic favoritism or procedural manipulation in favor of ruling-party constituencies.

In this section, we explore potential distributional consequneces of political alignment effect on reconstruction process discussed so far. Table 6 showcase these results which further illuminate the contextual factors that moderate the influence of political affili- ation on reconstruction program implementation.

The top panel highlights a distinct urban-rural divide in the influence of political alignment on beneficiary enrollment.
